message:
fix(fcp-oauth): reject empty refresh_token and id_token from OAuth responses

Hardens OAuthTokens against a malicious or misbehaving OAuth server that
returns refresh_token: "" or id_token: "" in a TokenResponse. Previously
these empty strings were stored verbatim as Some(String::new()), which:

1. Permanently broke the refresh loop: a compromised server could ship a
   200 OK with refresh_token: "" on any refresh call, overwriting the
   client's valid refresh token with a useless empty string. The next
   refresh attempt would send refresh_token="" and fail, forcing an
   interactive re-auth that never completes automatically.

2. Made is_refresh_valid() semantics ambiguous: Some("") presents as "we
   have a refresh token" to downstream code but is unusable on the wire.

Both OAuthTokens::from_response (initial token grant) and
OAuthTokens::update_from_response (refresh path) now filter refresh_token
and id_token through `.filter(|s| !s.is_empty())`, so empty strings are
normalized to None. On a refresh response that returns an empty
refresh_token the existing refresh_token is preserved unchanged — this
is the RFC 6749 §6 semantic for "no new refresh token issued", and it
matches what well-behaved servers send (field omitted, not empty).

Tests:

- test_from_response_rejects_empty_refresh_token: pins that an initial
  TokenResponse with refresh_token: Some("") and id_token: Some("")
  produces tokens.refresh_token() == None and tokens.id_token() == None.

- test_update_from_response_rejects_empty_refresh_token: pins that a
  refresh response with refresh_token: Some("") does NOT clobber a
  previously-valid refresh token — the original stored token survives.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
